Facts.text: Best view of Halley's Comet in 2,000 years
Battle of Abydos: Byzantine Emperor Basil II defeats and kills rebel general Bardas Phokas, ending his rebellion
Ransom of 48,000 pounds is paid to Vikings, led by Thorkell the Tall, for the liberation of Canterbury, after a raid on the city and the capture of Alphege [Aelfheah] Archbishop of Canterbury
German Bishop Gebhard of Dollnstein-Hirschberg named Pope Victor II
Papal bull "In nomine Domini" (In the Name of the Lord) issued by Pope Nicholas II establishing popes elected by Bishops and Cardinals not appointed by their predecessor
Diet of Gelnhausen; Henry the Lion deprived of the Duchy of Saxony
Crusaders occupy Constantinople during the Fourth Crusade
The Grand Union of the Augustinian order formed when Pope Alexander IV issues papal bull Licet ecclesiae catholicae
Pope Clement VI declares German emperor Louis of Bavaria, envoy
Ottoman army occupies Cairo
Portuguese converted Christian Marranos who revert to Judaism burned by order of Pope
Edict of Nantes grants political rights to French Huguenots
English Short Parliament forms (till May 5)
Dutch people protest poor quality of bread
Royal Military Academy forms at Woolwich, England
France defeats a European alliance in the Battle of Bergen
First elephant arrives in US from India
The Roman Catholic Relief Act passed by the British Parliament; it was the culmination of the process of Catholic Emancipation throughout the UK.
HMS Beagle anchors at river mouth of Rio Santa Cruz, Patagonia
William Henry Lane ("Juda") perfects tap dance
Lord Rosse successfully casts 72" (183-cm) mirror for a telescope
Battle of Irish Bend, Louisiana (Fort Bisland)
Society for the Relief of the Ruptured and Crippled is incorporated in the State of New York
Raleigh, North Carolina, captured by Union forces
Metropolitan Museum of Art forms in NYC, opening in the Dodworth Building at 681 Fifth Avenue
Colfax Massacre in Grant Parish, Louisiana (60 blacks killed)
Anti-Semitic League forms in Prussia
US prospector Alfred Packer convicted of manslaughter though accused of cannibalism

A squadron of the Russian fleet is decoyed out of Port Arthur by Japanese maneuvers, when they realize they are sailing into a trap; their battleship Petropavlovsk hits a mine and sinks, with a loss of 700 men
Battle of Oviumbo (in modern Namibia): Herero tribesmen rise up against German colonists
Mutiny on Portuguese battleships Dom Carlos & Vasco da Gama
Groundbreaking for Philadelphia's Shibe Park, home of MLB Athletics (AL), 1909-54, MLB Phillies (NL), 1938-70, and NFL Eagles, 1940-57
In Constantinople the primarily Albanian First Army Corps seizes the parliament building and telegraphs offices, forcing the Ottoman statesman Hilmi Pasha to resign
Polo Grounds grandstand & left field bleachers go up in flames in Manhattan, New York City
The US House of Representatives votes to institute direct elections of senators to Congress, a step towards direct democracy
Royal Flying Corps forms (later Royal Air Force)
1st Federal League Game: Baltimore Terrapins beat Buffalo Blues 3-2
Electrical fire kills 38 mental patients at Oklahoma State Hospital
British troops open fire on demonstrators in Amritsar, India, killing 350
Establishment of the Provisional Government of the Republic of Korea
1st woman US Civil Service Commissioner, Helen Hamilton appointed
Foundation of the Spanish Communist Workers' Party.
US Army wins 1st college three-weapon fencing championships
Greek plebiscite for a republic
Virginia Theater (ANTA, Guild) opens at 245 W 52nd St NYC
Cyclists without bicycle-tax-stamp rounded up in Amsterdam
Stanley Cup Final, Ottawa Auditorium, Ottawa, ON: Ottawa Senators beat Boston Bruins, 3-1 for a 2-0-2 series win
1st trans atlantic flight Europe-US (Fitzmaurice-von Hunefeld-Köhl)
1st flight over Mount Everest (Lord Clydesdale)
4.7 million US families report receiving welfare payments
US Congress passes Johnson Debt Default Act
Ioannis Metaxas becomes Prime Minister of Greece
Clifford Goldsmith's play "What a Life" premieres at The Biltmore Theater, NYC
The Hindustani Lal Sena (Indian Red Army) is formed and vows to engage in armed struggle against the British.
W Saroyan's "My Heart's in the Highlands" premieres in NYC
American athlete Cornelius "Dutch" Warmerdam, using a bamboo pole, becomes 1st man to pole vault 15 ft, at University of California, Berkeley
Second battle of Narvik; 3 German destroyers and one U-boat sunk by the Royal Navy, 5 more German destroyers scuttled
Stanley Cup Final, Maple Leaf Gardens, Toronto, Ontario: New York Rangers beat Toronto Maple Leafs, 3-2 for 4-2 series victory; Rangers last Cup win for 54 years
Heavy German assault on Tobruk
Pact of neutrality between the USSR and Japan is signed.
Catholic University Nijegen closes
Nazis discover mass grave of Polish officers near Katyn
New Zealand and the Soviet Union establish diplomatic relations
South Carolina rejects black suffrage
Stanley Cup Final, Montreal Forum, Montreal, Quebec: Montreal Canadiens beat Chicago Blackhawks, 5-4 in overtime for a 4-0 series sweep; Canadiens' first SC triumph since 1931
Canadian soldier Léo Major single-handedly liberates Dutch town of Zwolle by fooling Germans into thinking a raid had begun
WWII: Canadian army liberates Teuge & Assen, Netherlands, from Nazis
WWII: Russian Red Army occupies Vienna, Austria
WWII: US Marines conquer Minna Shima off Okinawa, Japan
Belgian Prime Minister Acker proclaims wage & price freeze ended
Eddie Klepp, a white pitcher signed by defending Negro League champ Cleveland Buckeyes, is barred from field in Birmingham, Alabama

Hadassah Convoy Massacre: Vehicles bringing Jewish staff and medical supplies to Hadassah Hospital and University on Mount Scopus, Jerusalem attacked by Arab forces; 79 killed by bombs, grenades, and sniper fire
During a lecture at the Mayo Clinic, Philip S. Hench reveals positive results of clinical trials using cortisone to treat rheumatoid arthritis
1st game of Milwaukee Braves, they beat Cincinnati Reds 2-0
Baltimore Orioles 1st game, loses to Tigers in Detroit 3-0
20.33" (51.64 cm) of rainfall, Axis, Alabama (then state record, eclipsed in 1997)
KETA TV channel 13 in Oklahoma City, OK (PBS) begins broadcasting
11th Tony Awards: "Long Day's Journey into Night" (play) & "My Fair Lady" (musical) win
Saturday postal delivery in US is temporarily halted due to lack of funds
12th Tony Awards: "Sunrise at Campobello" (play) and "The Music Man" (musical) win
American classical pianist Van Cliburn (23) wins the inaugural TInternational chaikovsky Competition in Moscow, USSR
USAF launches Discoverer II into polar orbit
Vanguard SLV-5 launched for Earth orbit (failed)
Vatican edict forbids Roman Catholics from voting for communists
US Department of Defense places "Transit 1B", 1st navigational satellite, in orbit around Earth
UN General Assembly condemns South-Africa's apartheid
US steel industry forced to give up price increases
Pirate's Bob Friend balks 4 times in a game
Ian Smith becomes Prime Minister of Rhodesia
New Zealander Colin Bosher shears a record 565 sheep in one workday
1st US Senate black page, Lawrence W Bradford Jr, 16, appointed by New York Senator Jacob Javits
7th Grammy Awards: "The Girl From Ipanema", The Beatles win
The Beatles record their single "Help!"
Pan Am places $525,000,000 order for 25 Boeing 747s
Closure of the Brisbane tramway network.
Apollo 13 announces, "Okay, Houston, we've had a problem here" after a Beech-built oxygen tank exploded en route to the Moon
Greek composer and counter-revolutionary Mikis Theordorakis freed from Oropos concentration camp, and allowed to be exiled to France
Oakland uses gold-colored bases during the club's home opener: Rules Committee subsequently bans this innovation
1st baseball players' strike ends after 13 days
Chad military coup by General Odingar
Christian Falange kills 27 Palestinians, begins Lebanese civil war
1st NBA playoff game for Cleveland Cavliers, they lose 100-95 to Washington
US Federal Reserve begins issuing $2 bicentennial notes
Jackson slugs a 3-run HR in the 1st inning, & the field is showered
Christian Turks occupy St Jansbasiliek
Longest doubles ping-pong match ends after 101 hours
Yusuf Lule becomes premier of Uganda
US and its allies boycott the Summer Olympics in Moscow in protest against Russia's invasion of Afghanistan
Pulitzer prize awarded to Beth Henley for "Crimes of the Heart"
Washington Post journalist Janet Cooke wins Pulitzer Prize for Feature Writing; she later admits story was a hoax and returns the prize
Undefeated middleweight boxer Tony Ayala gets 35 years on sex assault
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
11th NASA Space Shuttle Mission (41C): Challenger 5 returns to Earth
India beat Pak by 58 runs to win 1st Asia Cricket Cup in Sharjah
Montreal Expos infielderPete Rosebecomes 1st NL to get 4,000 hits in a career
"TASS" denounces US boycott of Moscow Olympics
Katrin Dörre wins 1st female World Cup marathon (2:33:30)
Space Shuttle Atlantis ferried to Kennedy Space Center via Ellington AFB, Texas
Boston Celtics end season with a 40-1 home win record
Spanish Grand Prix decided by 0.014 of a second
1st 3 San Diego Padres hit HRs off SF starter Roger Mason
Portugal signs agreement to return Macau to China (in 1999)
Italian government of Ciriaco De Mita forms
4th largest NBA crowd (45,458) see Orlando play at Minneapolis
NY Rangers beat NY Islanders 6-5, Rangers win preliminary, 4-1
BPAA US Open by Pete Weber
American Airlines reduce its 1st-class fares 20%-50%
Crystal Pepsi begins test marketing in Sacramento, Providence, Denver, Dallas, and Grand Rapids
Great Chicago Flood - Chicago's underground tunnels flood
Longest 2 undefeated baseball teams to meet (NY Yanks 5-0 vs Toronto Blue Jays 6-0); Yanks score 3 in top of 9th to win 5-2
Lou Carnesecca retires as coach of St John's basketball team
Magnitude 5.5 earthquake hits Netherlands
Presidential guard at Kigali, Rwanda, chops 1,200 church members to death
Target date for Israeli complete withdrawal, doesn't occur
United Arab Emirates' 1st official ODI, losing to India
Yankees beat the Mets 2-0
En route to NHL record 62 victories Detroit Red Wings win #61
Ottawa Senators eliminate Stanley Cup Champs NJ Devils from playoff
48th time opposing pitchers hit HRs, Carlos Perez (Mon)/Darren Holmes
Hartford Whalers last NHL game
Rare double doubleheader played in the same city Giants vs Mets & Oakland A's vs Yankees in NY after rain previous day
Pedro Carmona, interim president of Venezuela, resigns one day after taking office
Powerful tornadoes rip through Iowa City, Iowa.
North Korean long range rocket testing ends in failure after the rocket broke up after launch
20 civilians are killed by the government bombing of Saraqib, Idlib
8 people are killed after a bomb explodes on a bus in Peshawar, Pakistan
Kenyan Wilson Kipsang wins the London Marathon
Migrant ship carrying around 550 sinks off the Libyan coast, about 400 drown
Australian super-horse Winx ends extraordinary career with a 3rd Queen Elizabeth Stakes win in Sydney; 33 consecutive race wins, a world record 25 Group One victories and $26.4 million prize money
Body of 38-year-old Filipino woman discovered in abandoned mine starts the hunt for Cyprus's first serial killer, at least 5 other bodies later discovered
Gregg Popovich surpasses Lenny Wilkens to became the all-time winningest coach in NBA history with his 1,413th win as San Antonio Spurs beat Denver Nuggets, 101-96 in Western Conference Playoffs
World's largest plane by wingspan at 117m (385 ft), the Stratolaunch, built as a flying launch pad for satellites, takes its first flight from Mojave, California
US, South Africa and the EU authorities temporarily stop administrating Johnson & Johnson COVID-19 vaccines after six women developed blood clots (out of 6.8 million)
At least 448 people killed after heavy rains and flooding in KwaZulu-Natal province, South Africa, "one of the worst weather storms in the history of our country" according to authorities
American F.B.I. arrests 21-year-old member of Massachusetts Air National Guard for leaking classified documents, including national security secrets, on an online gaming chat group
Tropical Cyclone Ilsa makes landfall in remote western Australia, between De Grey and Pardoo Roadhouse, as a category five storm, setting a new sustained wind speed record of 218 kph (135 mph)
Iran launches a strike on Israel with 300 drones and missiles in retaliation for the bombing of its embassy in Damascus - almost all shot down
Six people are killed in a stabbing at a Bondi shopping mall in Sydney, Australia, by an attacker with mental health issues who is shot dead by police
Russian missile strike hits Ukrainian city of Sumy, killing at least 34 people and injuring more than 100 in the worst attack on civilians in 2025 so far

The MacLean brothers, Ewan (32), Jamie (31), and Lachlan (27) of Scotland depart in a rowboat from Lima, Peru to cross the Pacifiic Ocean, headed for Australia; they hope to raise funds for clean water access in Madagascar through donations
After months of controversy, Pope Paschal II crownsHenry V, King of Germanyand Italy, as the Holy Roman Emperor
The Seventh Crusade is defeated in Egypt, andLouis IXof France is captured
Raphael's final influential masterpiece "The Transfiguration" is put on display a week after the artist's death
Giovanni, son of Italian mathematicianGirolamo Cardanois executed for poisoning his wife, despite his father's efforts to save him
John Dryden, at age 36, is appointed the first English Poet Laureate by KingCharles II
George Frideric Handel's oratorio "Messiah" is performed for the first time at the New Music Hall in Dublin
British ship Endeavour captained byJames Cook, arrives in Matavia Bay, Tahiti, with botanistJoseph Bankson board
Battle of Millesimo, Italy:Napoleon's forces defeat allied armies of Austria and of the Kingdom of Sardinia-Piedmont.
Steam power brake patented  by American engineerGeorge Westinghouse
John Philip Sousa's "El Capitan" premieres at the Tremont Theatre in Boston
US Congress authorizesLewis and ClarkExposition $1 gold coin
At 38,Walter Johnsonpitches his 7th opening day shutout
Stanley Cup Final, Maple Leaf Gardens, Toronto, ON: New York Rangers beat Toronto Maple Leafs, 1-0 in OT for a 3-1 series win; first best-of-4 Finals series
FDRdedicates the Jefferson Memorial in honor of former PresidentThomas Jefferson

Transport No. 71 departs Drancy (France) internment camp, taking 1,500 French Jews toAuschwitzconcentration camp in Nazi German occupied Poland; up to 1,260 killed upon arrival, an estimated 130 survived
Milwaukee Braves'Hank Aaron's 1st game
Robert Oppenheimeraccused of being a communist
Stan Musialscores his 1,869th run, setting a new National League record
Cincinnati RedsPete Rosetriples for his 1st major league base hit
36th Academy Awards: "Tom Jones" (Best Film),Patricia Neal(Hud), andSidney Poitier(Lilies of the Field) win; Poitier is the first Black actor in a leading role to win a competitive Oscar
Ramiz Alia succeedsEnver Hoxhaas party leader of Albania
PopeJohn Paul IImeets Rome's Chief Rabbi Elio Toaff at Rome synagogue
Final episode ofPat Sajak's late night TV show on CBS
Tom Stoppard's play "Arcadia" premieres in London directed by Trevor Nunn, starring Rufus Sewell, Felicity Kendal and Bill Nighy
Asteroid 7373 Takei discovered and named after Star Trek actorGeorge Takei
Pedro Martíneztakes a perfect game through 7+1⁄3 innings until hitting Reggie Sanders with a pitch, leading to a bench-clearing brawl
NHL Pittsburgh PenguinMario Lemieux's last NHL regular game
"When Louis Met..." documentary byLouis Therouxpremieres on BBC2 in the UK featuring Jimmy Saville

Rock for the Rainforest benefit concert held at Carnegie Hall, NYC; performers include:Sting,Elton John,James Taylor,Stevie Wonder,Ricky Martin,Billy Joel,Gladys Knight, Percy Sledge,Tom Jones, Martha Reeves, Macy Gray, Sam Moore, and The Impressions

Rock for the Rainforest benefit concert held at Carnegie Hall, NYC; performers include:Sting,Elton John,James Taylor,Nina Simone,Smokey Robinson, Lulu, Patti Labelle,Ravi Shankar, Anoushka Shankar,Jeff Beck,Wynonna Judd, and Rebecca Del Rio
Barry Bondshits his 661st career home run, passing his godfather Willie Mays on the MLB all-time list
US record producerPhil Spectoris found guilty of second-degree murder of actress Lana Clarkson in 2003
Gary Sinisenarrates the audiobook ofJohn Steinbeck's "Of Mice and Men"
Jason Mraz's fourth studio album, "Love Is a Four Letter Word", is released
Manny Pacquiaodefeats Timothy Bradley to regain his WBO welterweight boxing title
"Avengers: Age of Ultron", directed byJoss Whedon, starringRobert Downey Jr.,Chris Evans,Chris Hemsworth,Scarlett Johansson,Mark RuffaloandJeremy Renner, premieres in Los Angeles, California
BTSis the first K-pop band to perform on US TV show "Saturday Night Live"
Bernie Sandersendorses former rivalJoe Bidenfor president
Italian tenorAndrea Bocellisings a special Easter concert in the deserted Milan cathedral, accompanied by an organist
NY GovernorAndrew Cuomosays “I believe the worst is over if we continue to be smart,” about the COVID-19 pandemic in New York as death toll passes 10,000
Voice actorHank Azariawho is white apologizes for voicing Indian character Apu on "The Simpsons" for 30 years
First Pony Express rider reaches Sacramento, California
After 34 hours of bombardment, Fort Sumter surrenders to Confederates (US Civil War)
Abyssinian War ends as British and Indian troops capture Magdala and Ethiopian Emperor Tewodros II commits suicide
Australian General Election: the Commonwealth Liberal Party
Louis II, Duke of Bavaria, ruler of upper Baveria, born in Heidelberg, Germany (d. 1294)
Peter Faber, French Jesuit theologian (co-founder of Society of Jesus), born in Villaret, Duchy of Savoy Holy Roman Empire (d. 1546)
Elisabeth of Valois, daughter of King Henry II of France and 3rd wife of Philip II of Spain, born in Fontainbleau, France (d. 1568)
Christina of Holstein-Gottorp, Queen consort of Sweden as the wife of King Charles IX, born in Kiel, Germany (d. 1625)
Albert VI, Duke of Bavaria, born in Munich (d. 1666)
Thomas Wentworth, 1st earl of Strafford and Viceroy of Ireland, born in London, England (d. 1641)
Roger de Rabutin, Comte de Bussy, French writer, born in Epiry, France (d. 1693)
Aert Jansse van Nes, Dutch naval commander, born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1693)
Jeanne Guyon, French mystic, born in Montargis, Orléanais (d. 1717)
Pierre Jélyotte, French composer, born in Lasseube, France (d. 1797)
Thomas Percy, English literary and bishop of Dromore, born in Bridgnorth, Shropshire, England (d. 1811)
Isaac Low, American delegate to the Continental Congress, born in Piscataway, Province of New Jersey, British America (d. 1791)
Louis Philippe II, Duke of Orléans, born in Château de Saint Cloud, Saint-Cloud, France (d. 1793)
Joseph Bramah, British inventor and locksmith who invented the beer pump, born in Stainborough, Yorkshire, England (d. 1814)
Frederik Sigismund van Bylandt, Dutch count and vice-admiral, born in Wageningen, Netherlands (d. 1828)
Louis Henri, Prince of Condé, born in Hôtel de Condé, Paris (d. 1830)

Johanna Cornelia Wattier-Ziesenis, Dutch actress (Phaedra, Lady Macbeth), born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1827)
Laurent, 1st Marquis of Gouvion Saint-Cyr, French marshal, born in Toul, Three Bishoprics, France (d. 1830)
Thomas Lawrence, English portrait painter, born in Bristol, England (d. 1830)
Frans Adam van der Duyn van Maasdam, Dutch governor (South Holland), born in Deventer, Netherlands (d. 1848)
Richard Trevithick, English inventor (steam locomotive), born in Tregajorran, England (d. 1833)
Eli Terry, American clockmaker whose manufacturing techniques turned clocks into everyday objects, born in East Windsor, Connecticut (d. 1852)[1]
Alexander Mitchell, Irish engineer (inventor of the screw-pile lighthouse), born in Dublin (d. 1868)
Cornelis Smit, Dutch ship builder, born in Alblasserdam, Netherlands (d. 1858)
Friedrich Graf von Wrangel, Prussian field marshal, born in Stettin, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1877)
John Robertson, American politician, born in Petersburg, Virginia (d. 1873)
Félix de Mérode, Belgian nobleman, Earl of Merode, Belgian minister of War and Finance, born in Maastricht, Dutch Republic (d. 1857)
Joseph Rastrelli, German composer, born in Dresden, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1842)
Ludwig Rellstab, German music theorist, born in Berlin, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1860)
Leopold Fitzinger, Austrian zoologist, born in Vienna (d. 1884)
Antonio Meucci, Italian inventor (invented a telephone-like device), born in Florence, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (now Italy) (d. 1889)
Henry Haywood Bell, U.S. Rear Admiral (Union Navy), born in Orange County, North Carolina (d. 1868)
William Sterndale Bennett, English composer, born in Sheffield, Yorkshire, England (d. 1875)
Alphonse Wauters, Belgian historian and archivist (Brussels), born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1898)
Leroy Augustus Stafford, Confederate American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born near Cheneyville, Rapides Parish, Louisiana (d. 1864)
William Stephen Walker, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1899)
Thomas D'Arcy McGee, Irish-Canadian journalist and Father of Confederation, born in Carlingford, County Louth, Ireland (d. 1868)
J. B. Lightfoot, English theologist and bishop of Durham, born in Liverpool, Lancashire (d. 1889)
Josephine Butler (née Grey), British feminist and social reformer (campaigned against the slave trade and Contagious Diseases Acts), born in Milfield, Northumberland, England (d. 1906)
Eduard Lassen, Belgian-Danish composer, born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1904)
James Wimshurst, British designer and inventor (electrostatic generator), born in Poplar, England (d. 1898)
Juan Montalvo, Ecuadorian author (Siete Tratados), born in Ambato, Ecuador (d. 1889)
Louis-Ernest Barrias, French sculptor, born in Paris (d. 1905)
Cornelis Pijnacker Hordijk, Dutch jurist and Governor-General of the Dutch East Indies (1888-93), born in Wadenoijen, Netherlands (d. 1908)
Enrique José Varona, Cuban author, born in Puerto Principe, Cuba (d. 1933)

Robert Abbe, American surgeon and radiologist, born in New York City (d. 1928)

James Ensor, Flemish painter and etcher (De lampenjongen), born in Ostend, Belgium (d. 1949)
Heinrich Reinhardt, Austrian composer, born in Pressburg, Austrian Empire (now Bratislava, Slovakia) (d. 1922)

Sammy Woods, Australian cricket all-rounder (3 Tests Australia, 3 England, 1 x 50, 10 wickets; Somerset CCC) and rugby union flanker (13 caps England, 5 as captain), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 1931)
John Blackwood McEwen, Scottish classical composer, born in Hawick, Scotland (d. 1948)
Alexander Roda Roda, Austrian writer, born in Drnowitz, Moravia (d. 1945)

Theodore F. Morse, American composer, born in Washington, D.C. (d. 1924)
Ray Lyman Wilbur, American politician and 3rd President of Stanford University, born in Boonesboro, Iowa (d. 1949)
Charles Christie, Canadian film studio owner, born in London, Ontario, Canada (d. 1955)
Demjan Bednyi, Russian writer and satirist, born in Hubivka, Ukraine (d. 1945)
Georg Lukács, Hungarian philosopher and literary critic, born in Budapest, Austria-Hungary (d. 1971)
Pieter Sjoerds Gerbrandy [Gerbrandij], Dutch lawyer, resistance fighter, and Prime Minister of the Netherlands (1940-45), born in Goënga, Netherlands (d. 1961)
Vean Gregg, American baseball player, born in Chehalis, Washington (d. 1964)
Ethel Leginska [Liggins], British pianist, conductor, composer, and music educator, born in Hull, Yorkshire, England (d. 1970)

Herbert Yardley, American cryptologist, born in Worthington, Indiana (d. 1958)
Frank Murphy, American politician and 56th United States Attorney General, born in Harbor Beach, Michigan (d. 1949)
Maurice Buckley, Australian soldier and winner of the Victoria Cross, born in Melbourne, Victoria (d. 1921)
Nellallitea "Nella" Larsen [Nellie Walker], African-American Harlem Renaissance novelist (Quicksand, Passing), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1964)
Arthur Harris, British RAF Commanding Chief known as "Bomber/Butcher Harris" for commanding the bombing campaign against Nazi Germany, born in Cheltenham, Gloucestershire (d. 1984)
Ludvig Irgens-Jensen, Norwegian composer, born in Oslo, Norway (d. 1969)
Arthur Fadden, 13th Prime Minister of Australia, born in Ingham, Queensland, Australia (d. 1973)
Olga Rudge, American concert violinist, born in Youngstown, Ohio (d. 1996)
Werner Voss, German World War I flying ace, born in Krefeld, Germany (d. 1917)
Alexander Alexandrovich Davidenko, Soviet composer, born in Odessa, Russian Empire (d. 1934)
Alfred Mosher Butts, American architect and game inventor (Scrabble), born in Poughkeepsie, New York (d. 1993)
Alfred Schütz, Austrian-American architect and philosopher, born in Vienna, Austria-Hungary (d. 1959)
Pierre Molinier, French painter and photographer, born in Agen, France (d. 1976)
Jacques Lacan, French psychoanalyst and semanticist, born in Paris (d. 1981)
René Pleven, Prime Minister of France (1950-52), born in Rennes, France (d. 1993)
Del Porter, American jazz singer (Foursome - "Walkin' My Baby Back Home"; Spike Jones and His City Slickers - "Der Fuehrer's Face"), born in Newberg, Oregon (d. 1977)
Godfrey Kenton, English actor (The Tempest; Well's Theater), born in London (d. 1998)
Philippe de Rothschild, French auto racer (Burgundy Grand Prix 1929) and wine grower (Château Mouton Rothschild), born in Paris, France (d. 1988)
Rex Evans, British actor (Frankenstein Meets The Wolfman; Zara; Matchmaker), born in Southport, Sefton, England (d. 1969)
Georges Yves Marie Congar, Dominican theologian cardinal, born in Sedan, Ardennes, France (d. 1995)
(Lawrence) "Bud" Freeman, American jazz tenor saxophonist, bandleader, and composer ("The Eel"; "Zen Is When"), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1991)
(Wilmore) "Slick" Jones, American jazz drummer (Fletcher Henderson, Fats Waller, Sidney Bechet), born in Roanoke, Virginia (d. 1969)
Harold Stassen, American politician (25th Governor of Minnesota) and perennial presidential candidate, born in Saint Paul, Minnesota (d. 2001)
Roderich Menzel, Czech tennis player (French Open singles 1938 runner-up; US Open mixed doubles 1935 runner-up), born in Reichenberg, Czech Republic (d. 1987)
Eudora Welty, American novelist (Optimist's Daughter-Pulitzer 1973), born in Jackson, Mississippi (d. 2001)
Mervyn Hugh Cowie, wildlife conservationist, born in Nairobi (d. 1996)
Ico Hitrec, Croatian footballer, born in Zagreb, Austria-Hungary (d. 1946)
Jean-Louis Lévesque, Canadian entrepreneur and philanthropist, born in Nouvelle, Quebec, Canada (d. 1994)
Nino Sanzogno, Italian conductor (La Fenice) and composer, born in Venice, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1983)
Roy Winsor, American producer (Search for Tomorrow, Love of Life), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1987)
Bernard Chacksfield, British Air Vice-Marshal, born in Ilford, Essex (d. 1999)
Dave Albritton, American high jumper (Olympic silver 1936), born in Danville, Alabama (d. 1994)
Bert Peleman, Flemish writer, born in Puurs, Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1995)
Bob Devaney, American College Football Hall of Fame coach (National C'ship, University of Nebraska 1970, 71; University of Wyoming; career record 136–30–7), born in Saginaw, Michigan (d. 1997)
Roderic Coote, British Anglican bishop of Colchester (1966-87), born in Woking, England (d. 2000)[1]
Stephan Hermlin, German writer, born in Chemnitz, Germany (d. 1997)
Tom Greenshields, English sculptor, born in Devon, England (d. 1994)
Edna Lewis 'the Mother of Soul Food', American chef and author (elevated Southern cooking), born in Freetown, Virginia (d. 2006)[1]
Phyllis Fraser [Helen Brown Nichols], American actress, journalist, and co-founder of Beginner Books, born in Kansas City, Missouri (d. 2006)
A. L. [Audrey] Barker, English writer, born in St Pauls Cray, Kent, England (d. 2002)

Phil Tonken, American radio and television announcer, born in Hartford, Connecticut (d. 2000)
Roland Gaucher, French journalist, born in Paris (d. 2007)

Claude Cheysson, French politician (Minister of Foreign Affairs), born in Paris (d. 2012)
John LaPorta, American jazz clarinetist and composer, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2004)
Ken Nordine, American voice-over and recording artist (World Jazz), born in Cherokee, Iowa (d. 2019)
Liam Cosgrave, Irish politician (Fine Gael Party; President of Ireland, 1973-77), born in Castleknock, Dublin (d. 2017)
Marthe Cohn, French nurse and spy during World War Ⅱ, born in Metz, France (d. 2025)[1]
Roberto Calvi, Italian banker and director (Banco Ambrosiano), born in Milan, Italy (d. 1982)

Maxwell Henley Harris, Australian poet and publisher (Gift of Blood), born in Adelaide, Australia (d. 1995)
